working-policy: When the request is ambiguous, ask via AskUserQuestion before coding — do not guess.
Write a short plan / work-log before any code change; get approval for non-trivial work.
Minimal change only: stay within the requested scope, no drive-by refactors.
Verify with `git status` / `git diff` before finishing; report what changed.
Never commit on your own — present changes and let the user decide.
technical: Generated output must be deterministic: same input => same output.
Never overwrite a file the tool did not generate without explicit approval.
commit-format: Message format: `ref-<jira-number>: <type> - <short description>` (e.g. `ref-1433: chore - add make help target`).
Use standard Conventional Commits types only: feat, fix, docs, style, refactor, perf, test, build, ci, chore, revert.
The `ref-<number>` is the Jira ticket for task tracking; ask the user for it (or derive it from the branch name) if unknown.
Subject line MUST NOT exceed 150 characters (hard limit); keep it concise — do not pad it.
Description is imperative; add an optional body for details, separated by a blank line.
Do NOT add a Co-Authored-By or any author trailer.
module-registry: Each project keeps a module registry at `docs/module-registry.md`.
For every module, record: Purpose (what it develops), Files (key files + their role), Routes (route path -> screen/handler).
After delivering a feature, update the registry so future work can look up which files/routes belong to which module.
Keep entries stable and sorted; do not rewrite unrelated sections.
project-profile: The project's learned knowledge lives in `docs/project-profile.md`: framework + language (with versions), architecture/layers and how they connect, key flows (data/auth/navigation), important config/variables/tokens, and a catalog of reusable components/widgets WITH file paths.
FIRST task in a project (profile missing): run `learn-project` to build it before implementing. Check with `skillrunner status`.
If the profile EXISTS: reuse it — apply the cataloged components/patterns directly; do NOT re-scan the whole source to rediscover them.
Rebuild the profile ONLY when the user explicitly asks to update it (re-run `learn-project`); never auto-refresh.
Safety: trust the profile for orientation, but cheaply confirm a specific file/symbol still exists before relying on it.

plan-feature.description: Turn a feature request into a reviewable plan + measurable goals — no code yet. Use when the user wants an approach scoped/designed before implementing, weighs options, or asks 'plan this / how would you build X / what's the approach'. Keywords: plan, scope, approach, design, goals, options, before coding.
plan-feature.goal: Produce a reviewable plan; do NOT write code yet.
instructions: Restate the request in one sentence to confirm understanding.
List 2-4 measurable candidate goals for the user to pick from.
Draft a step-by-step plan naming the files that would change.
Call out risks, unknowns, and decisions only the user can make.
Present everything and STOP for the user's decision.
outputs: A plan document — no code changes

spec-from-source.description: Turn a Jira/Confluence issue, a Google Sheet, an OpenAPI/Swagger file, or an authenticated OpenAPI endpoint (e.g. an authswagger proxy URL) into a structured spec. Use when the user points at such a source and wants requirements / acceptance criteria / data models / endpoints / flow extracted before coding. Keywords: spec, requirements, acceptance criteria, Jira, Confluence, Google Sheet, Swagger, OpenAPI, import spec, endpoint.
spec-from-source.goal: A clear spec (requirements, acceptance criteria, models, endpoints, flow) before coding.
inputs: A Jira/Confluence link, a Google Sheet link, an OpenAPI/Swagger file path, or an authswagger endpoint URL (`<authswagger-base>/openapi.json?env=&spec=`)
For a project that keeps a target-projects registry (alias -> path + default env/group), resolve the alias there first instead of asking the user to restate env/group each time
instructions: For a Confluence page or Google Sheet link, prefer `sr fetch --from <url|alias>` FIRST (0-token deterministic bridge): it writes clean markdown to docs/specs/ and prints a digest — read that instead of the raw page/sheet. Configure auth once in `.skillrunner/fetch.json` (Confluence email+token env; Google service-account/refresh). Fall back to Atlassian MCP only if `sr fetch` is unavailable.
Otherwise read the source (parse the OpenAPI file, or GET the authswagger URL — it is already authenticated and its `servers`/`basePath` already point at authswagger's own proxy).

If the request names one or a few specific endpoint paths rather than 'the whole API', filter the fetched spec down to just those paths/operations before extracting — don't carry the full spec into the output.
Extract: requirements, acceptance criteria, data models, endpoints, UI flow.
List ambiguities and open questions explicitly.
Write the spec in the user's language; do not start implementation.
Present and STOP for confirmation before any code or write-back.
outputs: A structured spec document — no code changes

check-conflicts.description: Cross-check sources (test cases from Excel/CSV/Google Sheet, spec from Confluence/MD, mockup images, and the applied components/libraries) against the stack's UI/UX rules and surface CONFLICTS for the user to decide. Use when the user has multiple sources of truth (tests vs spec vs mockup vs code) and wants contradictions / coverage gaps found before building. Read-only: never writes to Jira/Confluence, never edits code. Keywords: conflict, cross-check, test cases, spec vs mockup, coverage gap, UI/UX rules, discrepancy.
check-conflicts.goal: A conflict report (rule vs source, with verbatim quotes + severity + proposed options) — STOP for the user to choose; nothing applied.
inputs: Test cases: a CSV file and/or a public Google Sheet link (may be both)
Spec: a Confluence link or an exported .md file
Optional: the components + libraries in use (code paths) — to check the design against real component APIs
instructions: For a Confluence page or Google Sheet, run `sr fetch --from <url|alias>` FIRST (0-token deterministic bridge → docs/specs/*.md); fall back to Atlassian MCP READ tools only if unavailable. READ-ONLY — never write anything back to Jira/Confluence.
Read every provided source: test cases (CSV / fetched sheet), the spec, any images (vision), and the applied components/libraries. If a source is missing, note the reduced coverage — do not guess.
Load the UI/UX rules to compare against: the `design-system` + `conventions` pack rules for the detected stack, plus docs/ui-ux-conflicts.md for the known conflict taxonomy (A1–E3).

Cross-check each source pair (test↔spec/AC, test/spec↔UI-UX rules, image↔spec, spec/image↔components/libraries, internal Excel duplicates, coverage gaps). Ground every finding in a verbatim quote from BOTH sides; if two supplied sources of the same thing (e.g. CSV vs Sheet) disagree, that is itself a conflict.
Rate each conflict by severity + confidence; do NOT inflate severity when evidence is weak — mark 'insufficient evidence' instead.

Report using the template in docs/ui-ux-conflicts.md §4: per conflict — type, sources, quotes, impact, a proposed default option, and the decision checkbox. Do NOT auto-decide; no side 'wins' by default.
Present the report and STOP for the user to choose. Apply nothing; write nothing to Jira/Confluence/code. If the user later picks resolutions, produce a draft they apply themselves.
outputs: A conflict report grouped by severity — no changes to any source, code, Jira, or Confluence

commit.description: Split the working changes into per-module commits with Conventional Commit messages tagged by a Jira ref. Use when the user is ready to commit, wants commit messages drafted, or says 'commit this / write the commit / group my changes'. Proposes text first; commits only after confirmation; never pushes. Keywords: commit, git commit, commit message, conventional commit, Jira ref, stage changes.
commit.goal: Reviewable, module-scoped commits in the `ref-<n>: <type> - ...` format — only after the user confirms.
inputs: The Jira ticket number(s) for the work (ask if unknown)
instructions: Run `git status` and `git diff` to understand what changed.
Determine the Jira ref: derive it from the branch name if possible, otherwise ask the user.
Group changes by module/feature; propose one commit per group.
Draft each message as `ref-<jira-number>: <type> - <short description>` (<=150 chars) using a valid Conventional Commit type.
Present ONLY the proposed commit message text(s) — do NOT dump file diffs. STOP for the user's confirmation.
Commit only after the user confirms. Do NOT push — the user pushes themselves.
Do not append any author/Co-Authored-By trailer.
outputs: The proposed commit message text(s) for confirmation, then the commits (not pushed)

refactor.description: Improve a component/module for clarity and reuse WITHOUT changing behavior. Use when the user says clean up / tidy / simplify / dedupe / extract / reduce nesting on existing code and behavior must stay identical. Keywords: refactor, clean up, tidy, simplify, dedupe, DRY, extract, reduce nesting, no behavior change.
refactor.goal: Cleaner code, identical behavior, minimal diff.
instructions: Read the target and identify duplication, dead code, deep nesting.
Apply early-returns, extract reused pieces, prefer existing shared components.
Do NOT change observable behavior or public signatures without approval.
Run the stack linter and report the before/after diff.

explain-lib.description: Explain how THIS project actually uses a given library (no invented APIs). Use when the user asks how X is used here / where is X used / what's the pattern for X / how do we do X in this repo — grounded in real call sites, not generic docs. Keywords: how do we use, explain library, usage pattern, where is X used, real call sites.
explain-lib.goal: Accurate what/where/pattern/pitfalls, grounded in real usage.
instructions: Read the project's curated library docs (library-docs pack rules) first.
Find real call sites in the codebase to confirm usage.
Summarize: what it's used for, where, the standard pattern, common pitfalls.
Do not invent APIs — if unknown, say so.

check-diff.description: Review the current git diff against the stack's conventions. Use when the user wants pending changes checked before committing, or says 'review my changes / check my diff / did I follow conventions / lint check'. Reports file:line violations + runs the linter. Keywords: review diff, check changes, convention check, lint, pre-commit review, code review.
check-diff.goal: Report convention violations with file:line, then run the linter.
instructions: Run `git diff` to get the pending changes.
Check each change against the conventions rules for this stack.
Report violations as `file:line — rule` with a suggested fix.
Run the stack linter and include its results.

learn-project.description: Deep-dive a project ONCE and cache its architecture in docs/project-profile.md (reused later, refreshed only on explicit update). Use as the FIRST task in an unfamiliar project, or when the user asks to understand / map / onboard the codebase and no project-profile.md exists yet. Keywords: learn project, onboard, understand codebase, map architecture, first task, project profile, get up to speed.
learn-project.goal: A reusable profile: framework, language, architecture, flows, config, and a catalog of reusable components with paths.
instructions: Run `skillrunner status`: if docs/project-profile.md already exists, STOP unless the user explicitly asked to update it.
Detect the stack and load the matching pack rules for context.
Analyze the project: framework + language (with versions), architecture/layers and how they connect, key flows (data/auth/navigation), important config/env/variables/design tokens.
Catalog reusable components/widgets: name, file path, purpose, and how to use — so future tasks apply them without re-reading source.
Write docs/project-profile.md in a stable, structured format. Do NOT modify source code.
Summarize what was learned.
outputs: A short summary of the project's architecture

update-module-registry.description: Create or update the project's module registry (docs/module-registry.md). Use after touching a module's files/routes, or when the user asks to record / update what a module contains so future work can look it up. Keywords: module registry, record module, update registry, module map, files, routes.
update-module-registry.goal: Keep an accurate map of module -> purpose, files, and routes for future lookup.
inputs: The module that was touched and the files/routes added or changed
instructions: Read docs/module-registry.md (create it if missing).
Find or add the section for the touched module.
Record Purpose (what it develops), Files (key files + role), Routes (path -> screen/handler).
Keep entries sorted and stable; leave unrelated sections untouched.
Report which module sections changed.

deliver-feature.description: Deliver a Jira feature end to end: locate module -> plan -> goal -> code -> check -> clean -> verify -> commit. Use when the user gives a Jira task + description and wants it implemented start to finish; the orchestrator that chains learn-project, plan-feature, check-diff and commit. Keywords: deliver feature, implement ticket, Jira task, end to end, build the feature, ship it.
deliver-feature.goal: A verified, convention-clean feature with the module registry updated and a confirmed commit (not pushed).
instructions: If the Jira code or problem description is missing, ask for it.
Run `skillrunner status`. If docs/project-profile.md is missing, run `learn-project` first; otherwise reuse it — do not re-scan the whole source.
Consult CLAUDE.md, docs/project-profile.md, and docs/module-registry.md to locate the relevant module, its files, routes, and reusable components.
Produce a plan and 2-4 measurable goals; present them and STOP for the user's approval before coding.
Implement the code/components per the stack conventions and design system; reuse existing components first.
Run check-diff against conventions and run the linter; clean/refactor the touched components without changing behavior.
Update docs/module-registry.md for the module (purpose / files / routes).
Verify the change end to end (drive the affected flow, not just build/lint).
Propose the commit message(s) as `ref-<jira>: <type> - <desc>` (<=150 chars) TEXT ONLY — no file dumps — and STOP for confirmation.
Commit only after the user confirms. Do NOT push — the user pushes themselves.

scaffold-data.description: Build the data layer for an API call following the stack's architecture. Use when the user wants an endpoint integrated — models/DTOs + service/repository/provider + wiring — but NOT UI. Keywords: data layer, API call, integrate endpoint, model, DTO, service, repository, provider, fetch data.
scaffold-data.goal: Types/models + service/provider + wiring, per the pack's templates.
inputs: Endpoint, method, request/response shape (ask if unknown)
instructions: Confirm the endpoint contract; ask for missing request/response shapes.
Copy the pack's data-layer template (do not invent a new structure).
Generate the model/types (null-defensive) and the service/provider.
Wire it into the repository/hook layer as the architecture requires.
Do NOT build UI here; stop at the data layer.
outputs: Data-layer files following the pack conventions

scaffold-screen.description: Scaffold a new screen/feature following the stack's architecture. Use when the user wants a new page/screen created (list / detail / form / dialog) split into the stack's layers and wired into routing. Keywords: new screen, new page, scaffold feature, list screen, detail screen, form, dialog, route.
scaffold-screen.goal: A screen split into the pack's layers, wired into routing.
inputs: Screen name, purpose, whether it is a list/detail/form
instructions: Pick the matching pack template (list / detail / form / dialog).
Generate the layers the architecture requires (view / logic / data split).
Register the route per the routing convention.
Follow all naming and structure conventions from the pack.

build-ui.description: Build or edit UI using the stack's design system and tokens. Use when the user wants a component/layout built or changed, reusing shared components and semantic tokens (no hardcoded colors/spacing). Keywords: build UI, edit UI, component, layout, design system, design tokens, styling, restyle.
build-ui.goal: UI that reuses shared components and obeys design tokens.
inputs: The UI to build/change and where it lives
instructions: Reuse existing shared components before creating new ones.
Use semantic design tokens — never hardcode colors/spacing.
Follow the design-system pack patterns for the component type.
Keep behavior and accessibility feedback (loading/success/error) visible.
outputs: UI files using shared components and tokens

gen-routes.description: Generate/refresh route entries from the project's file structure. Use when the user wants routing regenerated or synced after adding/removing pages, following the stack's file-to-route convention. Keywords: routes, routing, generate routes, refresh routes, route map, register route.
gen-routes.goal: Deterministic file -> route mapping following the convention.
inputs: The routing convention and the pages/routes directory
instructions: Scan the configured directory for route files.
Derive each route path from the convention.
Write the routes file with stable, sorted output.
Report added/removed/changed routes as a diff summary.
